title: Survey of Multiagent Systems for Improving Home Health Care Management
research_objective: Review agent-based approaches for operational home-healthcare management
agent_architecture_and_autonomy: Mostly distributed scheduling and agent-based simulation; limited deployed autonomy
explainability: Explains system features and evaluation criteria rather than AI decisions
interoperability: Limited EHR mention; no standards-based integration
governance_and_oversight: End-user provision and practical evaluation treated as deficiencies
sdlc_coverage: Requirements and implementation availability are observed; no autonomous SDLC
evaluation_and_data: Ten approaches compared using concept; implementation; and evaluation features
main_findings: MAS supports decentralized home-care planning but no reviewed approach was suitable for practical application
limitations_and_future_work: Implementations were often unavailable and evaluations weak; reimplementation and objective assessment were recommended

title: Application of Intelligent Multi Agent Based Systems for E-Healthcare Security
research_objective: Propose intelligent agent-based access control for secure EHR sharing
agent_architecture_and_autonomy: Agents divide authentication; authorization; access control; and data-sharing tasks
privacy_and_security: Agent-mediated access control for EHR confidentiality and authorized sharing
explainability: No explanation mechanism for agent or access-control decisions
interoperability: EHR-focused; no FHIR or HL7 implementation
governance_and_oversight: Roles are assigned to agents; consent and human approval are not operationalized
sdlc_coverage: Security design only; no development lifecycle automation
evaluation_and_data: No real-time implementation; efficiency and accuracy left for future work
main_findings: Shows how MAS can separate security responsibilities around EHR access
limitations_and_future_work: Conceptual model lacks implementation and empirical validation
relevance: High for privacy; low for explainability

title: Quantum Diffie--Hellman Extended to Dynamic Quantum Group Key Agreement for e-Healthcare Multi-Agent Systems in Smart Cities
research_objective: Secure group communication among healthcare stakeholders against quantum-capable adversaries
healthcare_setting: Smart-city e-healthcare; zonal sensor and stakeholder architecture
agent_architecture_and_autonomy: Three-layer zonal architecture with communicating agents and a group controller
privacy_and_security: Quantum group-key agreement; join/leave protocols; internal and external attack analysis
explainability: No user-facing explanation or rationale mechanism
interoperability: Healthcare data exchange architecture; no FHIR implementation
governance_and_oversight: Controller responsibilities are specified; governance and human approval are outside scope
sdlc_coverage: Cryptographic runtime mechanism; no SDLC coverage
evaluation_and_data: Protocol compared on qubit and unitary-operation efficiency; appointment dataset used for execution analysis
main_findings: Provides a specialized secure communication primitive for a healthcare MAS
limitations_and_future_work: Formal security model and real deployment remain future work; mechanism does not address broader privacy lifecycle
relevance: Medium for security; low for full topic

title: Privacy-Preserving Healthcare Data Interactions: A Multi-Agent Approach Using LLMs
authors: De Maio; Fenza; Furno; Grauso; Loia
venue: Journal of Communications Software and Systems
research_objective: Enable secure natural-language retrieval and updates of healthcare records
healthcare_setting: Clinical EHR query and update workflows
agent_architecture_and_autonomy: Orchestrated LLM agents route retrieval and update paths; includes a Data Update Agent and modular agent replacement
privacy_and_security: Privacy-preserving routing; access restrictions; controlled updates; audit-related handling
explainability: Agent outputs provide interaction-level reasoning but no artifact-level software rationale
interoperability: FHIR-based EHR access is central; DICOM and HL7 proposed as extensions
governance_and_oversight: Differentiated permissions and controlled actions; limited treatment of responsibility assignment and human gates
sdlc_coverage: Runtime EHR interaction; no automated requirements; coding; testing; or deployment agents
evaluation_and_data: Clinical scenarios; noisy inputs; complex queries; baseline comparisons; scalability and accuracy tests
main_findings: Demonstrates a concrete bridge between LLM agents and secure interoperable records
limitations_and_future_work: No real clinical deployment; multilingual; DICOM; HL7; and broader field validation remain future work
page_evidence: PDF pp. 1-9; evaluation pp. 6-8; conclusion pp. 8-9

title: Agentic-AI Healthcare: Multilingual, Privacy-First Framework with MCP Agents
research_objective: Combine MCP agent orchestration; multilingual interaction; privacy controls; and explainable responses
healthcare_setting: Symptom checking; medication suggestions; appointment scheduling
agent_architecture_and_autonomy: MCP coordinates symptom; medication; and appointment agents through a modular stack
privacy_and_security: RBAC; AES-GCM field encryption; tamper-evident audit logging; compliance layer
explainability: Transparent diagnostic reasoning examples; no formal faithfulness evaluation
interoperability: FHIR/EHR integration mentioned as future direction rather than implemented core
governance_and_oversight: Auditability and role controls are explicit; prototype disclaimer requires professional supervision
sdlc_coverage: Software architecture is described; no autonomous SDLC workflow
evaluation_and_data: Illustrative multilingual use cases and architecture demonstration; no controlled comparative evaluation
main_findings: Shows that privacy controls and agent orchestration can coexist in one prototype
limitations_and_future_work: Single-investigator preprint; no certified device; limited evaluation; agent authentication left for future work
page_evidence: PDF pp. 1-5; architecture pp. 2-4; conclusion p. 5

title: AI Agents in Clinical Medicine: A Systematic Review
research_objective: Evaluate clinical AI-agent performance; architecture patterns; and tool use
healthcare_setting: Diagnosis; evidence synthesis; treatment planning; operations; genomics; education
agent_architecture_and_autonomy: Single-agent tool use; multi-agent collaboration; and hybrid architectures; defines minimum agent capabilities
privacy_and_security: Privacy appears as an implementation concern; no unified mechanism is evaluated
explainability: Explainability receives limited direct analysis despite safety concerns
interoperability: EHR and external-tool access occur in included systems; no common FHIR requirement
governance_and_oversight: Calls for human oversight and safety evaluation; accountability mechanisms vary
evaluation_and_data: PRISMA-aligned; PROSPERO registered; 6023 records; 20 studies; QUADAS-AI appraisal; heterogeneous outcomes
main_findings: Agent architectures often improve base-LLM performance when matched to task complexity
limitations_and_future_work: Only 30 percent low risk across all bias domains; synthetic data and heterogeneous outcomes limit generalization; multicenter trials needed
page_evidence: PDF methods pp. 6-10; results pp. 10-23; discussion pp. 23-25

title: Multi-Agent AI Systems in Healthcare: Systematic Evidence Synthesis Via PRISMA of Clinical Decision Support Systems, Robotic Interventions, and Critical Care
authors: Ode; Kalu; Abbas; Arshad; Inalegwu; Koshechkin
research_objective: Synthesize MAS evidence across clinical decision support; robotic intervention; and critical care
agent_architecture_and_autonomy: BDI; fuzzy; reinforcement-learning; modular specialist-agent; and robotic-agent patterns
privacy_and_security: Privacy recognized as an ethical and deployment concern rather than consistently implemented
explainability: Transparency is recommended; few included systems provide strong explanation evidence
interoperability: EHR integration appears in examples; no shared interoperability architecture
governance_and_oversight: Only 7 of 32 studies addressed ethical or legal issues in depth; human interaction and accountability are comparison dimensions
evaluation_and_data: PRISMA; four databases; 150 records; 32 included; JBI appraisal; 20 simulation-based and 12 real-world implementations
main_findings: MAS can improve decisions and monitoring but deployment evidence and responsible design remain uneven
limitations_and_future_work: More than 60 percent lacked clinical validation; shared evaluation standards and interdisciplinary work are needed
page_evidence: PDF method pp. 2-3; findings pp. 3-7; conclusion p. 8

title: Multi-agent Artificial Intelligence Systems in Health Care: Applications, Challenges, and Future Prospects
authors: Saeed; Bazgha; Mukhtar; Akhtar; Faizi; Koshechkin
research_objective: Describe healthcare MAS applications; benefits; challenges; and future prospects
healthcare_setting: Decision support; data management; resource management; remote care
agent_architecture_and_autonomy: General autonomous and collaborative agent patterns across several healthcare domains
privacy_and_security: Discusses privacy; access control; encryption; federated learning; and security concerns
explainability: Calls for explainable systems and transparency but does not operationalize explanations
interoperability: Discusses EHR integration and legacy-system challenges; FHIR receives brief mention
governance_and_oversight: Addresses bias; accountability; human oversight; and regulatory compliance at a narrative level
evaluation_and_data: Narrative synthesis; no reproducible selection or quantitative evaluation protocol
main_findings: MAS may improve coordination and resource use; adoption depends on trust; standards; and governance
limitations_and_future_work: Broad scope; secondary claims; limited methodological traceability and no new deployment evidence
page_evidence: PDF pp. 1-13; challenges pp. 7-12; conclusion p. 14

title: Multiagent AI Systems in Healthcare: An Overview of Its Applications in Aspects of Healthcare
research_objective: Map MAS applications and implementation challenges in healthcare
healthcare_setting: Operations; decision-making; remote monitoring; emergency care; surgery; diagnosis
agent_architecture_and_autonomy: General collaborating-agent models; examples drawn from prior systems
privacy_and_security: Data security and patient privacy are listed as barriers; no implemented privacy layer
explainability: Explainability is mentioned as a trust concern
interoperability: Legacy interoperability and fragmented EHR data are major barriers; no FHIR implementation
governance_and_oversight: Calls for governance; fairness; and stakeholder acceptance
evaluation_and_data: Narrative domain comparison without reproducible review protocol
main_findings: MAS can coordinate distributed care tasks but legacy integration and governance constrain adoption
limitations_and_future_work: Relies on secondary evidence; no new system; no formal quality assessment

title: Multiagent Artificial Intelligence Systems for Decision Support in Healthcare: A Systematic Review
research_objective: Review MAS architectures; agent roles; AI methods; and decision domains
healthcare_setting: Chronic care; logistics; mental health; collaborative clinical decisions
agent_architecture_and_autonomy: Decentralized and adaptive decision-support agents; architectures vary across 12 selected studies
privacy_and_security: Secure communication and privacy are discussed as adoption requirements
explainability: Explainability is identified as an underdeveloped requirement
interoperability: EHR integration is discussed; no common FHIR implementation across reviewed systems
governance_and_oversight: User engagement; validation; and responsibility require further work
evaluation_and_data: Four databases; 110 initial records; 12 studies included; descriptive comparison
main_findings: MAS supports complex decision-making but evidence remains fragmented
limitations_and_future_work: System integration; validation; user engagement; and comparable metrics remain weak
page_evidence: PDF methods pp. 1-3; synthesis pp. 3-8; conclusion p. 9

title: Ethical Issues in Multi-Agent AI Systems for Healthcare: A Narrative Review
authors: Xie; Wang; Dai; Wang; Song; Qian
research_objective: Analyze ethical risks intensified by autonomous and interacting healthcare agents
healthcare_setting: Clinical and public-health uses of multi-agent AI
agent_architecture_and_autonomy: Distributed autonomous agents with planning; memory; and inter-agent reasoning
privacy_and_security: Complex data flows increase privacy and security risks; recommends privacy-centered design
explainability: Compound opacity; explanation needs; and patient comprehension are central
interoperability: Interoperability is not the primary focus
governance_and_oversight: Identifies error attribution; automation bias; erosion of oversight; consent threats; and contextual blindness; recommends adaptive governance
sdlc_coverage: No autonomous SDLC coverage; risks apply to generated artifacts by inference
evaluation_and_data: Narrative review of work from 2022-2025; seven ethical challenge categories
main_findings: MAS distributes decisions and can blur responsibility; technical safeguards need governance and clinician authority
limitations_and_future_work: No empirical intervention test; future work should operationalize governance in interdisciplinary pilots
page_evidence: PDF pp. 1-13; ethical analysis pp. 5-12; conclusion p. 13

title: A Systematic Review of Multi-Agent Systems in Healthcare: Applications, Challenges, and Strategic Approaches for Enhancing Clinical and Operational Outcomes
authors: Yusuf; Kalagbor; Ogegere; Tedongue; Eke; Ogegere; Koshechkin
venue: International Journal of Clinical and Medical Surgery
research_objective: Review MAS applications; benefits; challenges; and strategic adoption approaches
healthcare_setting: Diagnosis; monitoring; resource allocation; personalized treatment
agent_architecture_and_autonomy: Broad range of decentralized MAS applications
privacy_and_security: Privacy-by-design; blockchain; federated learning; and secure integration are proposed strategies
explainability: Explainability and trust are discussed at a broad level
interoperability: EHR integration and interoperability are recurring challenges
governance_and_oversight: Regulatory compliance; bias; stakeholder training; and accountability are discussed
evaluation_and_data: Reports PRISMA search across five databases from 2015-2025; heterogeneous evidence
main_findings: MAS shows promise across clinical and operational settings but adoption barriers remain
limitations_and_future_work: Heterogeneous designs and contexts prevent consistent conclusions; large-scale standardized research is recommended
page_evidence: PDF pp. 1-10; methods pp. 1-2; challenges pp. 6-9
